sentences.text: No, they came with me.
My mother and my sister arrived with me together.
Well, we came from Brunnlitz.
No.
My mother and my sister.
By that time I did not know yet about my wife.

But after that next day, or day after, I received information of people who used to tell everyone and anybody else that they have seen my wife, that she keeps together with her brother, sister, and sister-in-law.
And they are on their way back to Krakow.
Yes?
It was from the 15th of October, '44, till 7th of June.
That was two-- six-- eight weeks, or maybe close to nine, nine months.
Sorry.
I have to mention to you that on the 15th of October, when we were sent to Gross Rosen, I said to my wife, I want to say goodbye because we don't know what will happen after we are separated.
We might see each other.
We might not.
I anticipated something can happen.
And we decided to meet ourselves together after the end of the war, in three addresses.
Because we did not know which address will be available, which house will be intact.
So the first address was in the place where a very old and distinct widow after a judge prepared for us lunches when we are not allowed to go to public restaurant.
That was very close to the place where we worked.
If you wish, I can mention even the names, the street, where the widow was living.
It was Zielona, which is green, if I transfer it-- translate it in English.
The second address was the place where we worked, Sebastiano's.
It was the representation of-- oh my god, he sold watches, where we worked together.
My wife was working there together with me.
And the third address was [NON-ENGLISH].
That was the street where my wife was born or used to live before we married.
So when I arrived on the 1st of June, the first stop was I went to that old lady.
And I told her about our arrival.
And she allowed us to have one room and the kitchen behind the front.
And I told her, I have no idea where is my wife, but I heard that she is on the way.
After a few days-- it is on the 6th already, the 7th-- my wife arrived to Krakow station.
And she decided just the same.
The first thing was to go to this old lady on Zielona street.
And the, the lady took her with a great cry and laugh, and so on.
And Invited her into the front room.
And ask her how was it?
And my wife told her this and that.
After half an hour of talking, my wife decided to ask the lady one question.
Did you hear something about my husband?
And she said, yes!
He is here with the whole family, behind in the kitchen!
Now, you can imagine that moment of reunion.
So we decided to live again together, but we couldn't get our accommodation back, what we used to have before.
There was a Jewish students' home, not very far, which was now-- released by the Russian army.
They occupied, of course, all empty places.
And the Polish government allowed us to take-- for the time being-- some rooms.
So we all took our luggage from the old lady, and everybody got his room.
And so we started to live together.
We visited some Polish people.
They were very happy to see us.
But some of them said, with indignation, when my wife ask about a particular girl, if, if the-- how you called it?
In French they call it-- I forgot the name.
The lady who kept the eye on the cleaning of the room and so on.
And she said, you're asking me about that?
How do I know?
There are so many of you now, still back again.
Which shows you the anti-Semitic feelings, that now she could say, I don't know her.
But if she wouldn't say that we are here still, you are still in such a great amount.
They were started to be wondering, what's happened?
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And later on, the Polish authorities decided to leave this, this home for students.
And we had to slowly transfer ourselves wherever we wanted.
So we decided if we can't get our accommodation in Krakow, then we have picked a next city, which is close.
That was Biala and Bielsko.
They are well-known places in Polish history, especially that--
Pardon?
Biala was B-I-A-L-A.
And Bielsko is B-I-E-L-S-K-O.
They are centers for textile.
It was well known before the war, and it was continuing.
So we decided to stop there.
And the first person who we found on the street was a man who touched my arm.
And he said, hello.
How are you?
You remember me?
I said, yes.
That was one of the Polish people who were in Krakow-Plaszow.
And he said, I am here [NON-ENGLISH].
That means dignitary of the administration, Polish administration.
Not the town hall, in the big cities, but in a smaller one.
They were called some-- in Germany, burgermeister.
And he said, what are you doing?
I said, we just arrived, and we are trying to find out accommodation and work.
So he said-- and asked my wife what she was doing, and she was a clerk.
So he said, I employ you in my administration immediately.
And now I will send you to my friend, who is the chief of the Police.
He will give you somebody to go and find a proper accommodation.
And you will collect, also, furniture from the Germans who used to live there.
So thanks to that fact, very quickly we decided to sit on our furniture in, in a very nice building, which was the-- something similar like here, an insurance company building.
When you told me earlier that there was a move to migrate to Palestine, why didn't you take advantage of it?
First of all, we were tired from that trip.
And my wife was of the opinion that we have to wait and see how the things develop.
But nevertheless-- [CLEARS THROAT] --we registered ourself.
We didn't want to go first.
It came like-- because we left this Biala-Bielsko place, because our sister, my wife's sister, who was marrying a man in Krakow, stayed in Krakow.
She got an accommodation.
And they had a shop, like here, hardware and paints and so on.
And she decided to bring us back from Biala-Bielsko, to Krakow.
And they put an advert into the paper.
And she was successful.
She found a man who was living in Krakow, and he wanted to go back to Biala-Bielsko.
And we exchanged our accommodation.
Everything was going smoothly.
And when we came to Krakow back, I received a very good position in the taxation department.
And my wife, by that time, we had a child who was born in Bielsko, my Michael, one of those.
And I found a friend who was wondering why I was not looking for a public-- for a private employment.
By that time, my monthly wages was 10,000 Polish Zloty.
And a friend of mine told me, I will arrange, for you, a better position in private sector.
It is Dr.
[NON-ENGLISH], a very well-known person, who had, in the center of Krakow, in the main place, a book store, wholesale book.
And also, he was an editor of law literature, commentaries and so on.
And he offered me 40,000 zloty.
So immediately, I changed.
But that wasn't for a long time because later on, as I mentioned before, that came the order to pick up the trouble and go to Israel.

At first, the passport was-- or rather, it wasn't a passport, but it was a certificate that we are resigning of our citizenship in Poland, and we want to go to Israel, because there was no other way to go.
And my brother-in-law and my wife's sister got first that certificate.
They had to go.
And they had, also, a small girl.
So he left me his shop to liquidate.
I had to sell hardware, brushes, paints, and so on.
So apart from my problem and to work in that book shop, I had to do in my free time to liquidate.
Anyhow, later on, we received the papers.
And we were packing and going to Israel through Vienna.
It was a close transport from Vienna to Rome, and from Rome to Haifa.
Well, when we came to Haifa, we had to register again.
We had to go through Sharalea.
That is the place where all newcomer will be directed where to go.
We received, at the beginning, a place in, in, tents in [NON-ENGLISH].
But my wife was invited, with my son, to my sister-in-law's because they had an accommodation in Ra'anana.
So I spent one or two nights in [NON-ENGLISH] in the tent.
I received three beds, collapsible beds, three blankets, and some bread and so on.
So we were in Israel.
But the way-- the life in Israel, by that time--
And that was the year 1949.
It was very, very difficult.
A young country couldn't absorb and couldn't provide for everybody what we wanted.
We were always wondering, we are survivors from the Holocaust.
We had first rights, which was not true.
Anyhow, in the beginning, I had to go to work, to orchards, to [INAUDIBLE].
I cut down so many oranges, grapefruits.
You have no idea.
It was a very nice life there.
My wife, of course, didn't work.
Only kept the eye on my son.
And one day, when I was in Tel Aviv, I was stopped by one friend.
We used to play when we were small children in [NON-ENGLISH] what I mentioned before.
I know-- I knew him from that time.
And he said-- he asked me, what you doing?
I said, I didn't know.
I'm going to the [NON-ENGLISH] and so on.
But we have contacts in America and Australia.
Maybe that we will go out.
And he said, I, I won't allow you to leave Israel.
He immediately went to the telephone book.
He rang to his friend.
He said, listen, you have to do me a favor.
I saved your life.
Yes.
And you have to do me a favor.
I want you to give a job to my friend who just arrived.
So he said, let him come.
Because he said, he's a bookkeeper and so on.
One day I didn't go to the employment office because the rain was-- and to work in orchard when it's rain is impossible.
So I-- instead to go to work, I stopped the bus and I went to Netanya.
Netanya was a small place which was just starting to develop.
I had to go to Netanya.
And the man was waiting for me in the central bus stop, the [HEBREW].
He welcomed me very much.
We talked about this, about that.
And he said, listen, I promise my friend-- [NON-ENGLISH] was his name-- that I will keep an eye on you.
All right, listen.
Next week, you're going to Jerusalem, to [NON-ENGLISH], to learn Hebrew and find out what are the tax problems.
It will take some time.
So I will send to your wife every week or every month, money-- that will be a loan-- to keep her waiting for you.
When you finish the [INAUDIBLE], you come back.
You'll find out where your accommodation in my office.
Go now back and find out what your wife thinks.
I come to Ra'anana, and I told my wife all the story.
And she say, look, on the table is the lending permit to go to Australia.
[LAUGHS]
One son, Michael, was born in Krakow, in Bielsko.
Oh, yes.
Yes, because they wanted to re-privatize some houses, some properties their parents left and so on.
Oh yes, there were many of them.
There was the synagogue, or the temple.
It was used by Germans during the war as a store.
But lately, they would clean it up.
But there were only a handful of people who came to Mignon, very little.
I went once or twice when I was in Krakow.
But it was not like life which was before the war.
We found that's the past.
My wife was not brought up in that way.
And it was very hard to be kosher in every respect because it was costly.
No.
We rather tried to do what we could.
I was trying, when my mother passed away.
And it was here in Australia, that I contact the South [INAUDIBLE] Hebrew congregation, which was mostly of the people from Krakow, which I found.
And the way-- how they conducted the service was suitable.
There was Orthodox people that I joined them.
I'm am their member for the last 30 years.
Huh?
We left Israel on the 15th of August, I would say.
One moment.
We left Poland in '50, in November.
I was in Israel for the half of the next year because I had to liquidate that business of my brother-in-law.
And we had to go, then, with all the [INAUDIBLE] and all the formalities.
As a mother of fact, it was not easy.
But that lending permit was made for Paris.
And the idea was that we would go from Krakow to Paris, which was not very easy.
So when I came to the Australian embassy in Tel Aviv, and I described the situation, they said, we will accept the lending permit made for Tel Aviv.
Go ahead with all the formalities and so on.
So we-- our sister-in-law, who was here earlier than we-- the second sister.
One sister was with my wife.
The other sister was with her husband.
She was in Germany still.
But they came half a year before we, to Australia.
They prepaid for us, the, the the fees for the way.
We had to pay from Haifa through-- oh, to Bombay.
And from Bombay, by ship to Haifa.
So we arrived really here on the 6th of August, 1950, to Australia.
Yes, partly.
But more, I understood.
When I was in Israel, I didn't waste time.
I took lessons from a very good teacher, twice or three times weekly.
So when we came-- and my wife used to know English from the time when she was a girl in Krakow.
There was a fashion, to have knowledge of some languages, N she knew a little bit of English.
But anyway, we succeeded to travel with that knowledge.
And when we arrived here to Australia, I continued the evening courses.
And my wife received correspondence courses in English.
So after two, three years, we were able to read, and converse, and take some positions here.
When I came to Australia, we came to our brother and sister, in their home in Pascoe Vale.
It was in the northern part of big Melbourne.
And I was looking for a position.
And by that time, a very close place where I could work was Lincoln Mills, a place spin the rye, or I don't know the English name.
It was a night, night, night, nighttime job.
I had to come to work at 8:00 or something like this, work over the night, and six o'clock, went home.
But it was for me, after one week of work and after-- it was impossible.
I never worked in the, in the night.
Of course, during the war we had nights sleepless and so on.
But to be able to work consistently in a factory, I said, no, that is not for me.
I transfer the job.
And I started to work in daytime as a store man in shoe factories.
I tried, of course, to improve the wages.
I went through several shops, several factories.
But I came to the conclusion that this is not my future, that I have to continue my bookkeeping accountancy, which I knew when I was working in Krakow.
And during one bar mitzvah simcha, I met one gentleman who told me that he knows somebody who would employ me.
But I have to see him and so on.
It took me time to decide.
Because in the meantime, I had to paint my accommodation.
And during interval, when I looked through the I found an advert that a Jewish girl would be necessary for a small timber mill office.
And the name-- the address was in [NON-ENGLISH].
So I rang my friend in [NON-ENGLISH].
And I said-- I knew that she has got a small child that he is parading with that pram here and there.
That I had that address where I should inquire.
So I ask her, go there and ask the man who put the advert if instead of a Jewish girl, if he could employ a Jewish boy with the same wages what a girl.
Because by that time, the girl was receiving 75% the wages.
So my friend went there and they say, all right, we would like to see the man, because she described as a survivor from Holocaust.
And I brought some credentials.
I made a good impression.
And they say, all right, we employ you.
But the work was a little bit far from my place of residence.
We lived by eastern Kilda.
I had to go by train to Richmond, change the line and go to Mitchum, which was close to Ringwood.
One hour there, one hour there.
And after that time, when I heard that there is a chance to get the position in the city, I went to that man.
And that was a well-known philanthropic, Mr.
Tannenbaum.
He was well known in the Jewish Welfare Society.
He approved my application, and I started to work in the office.
The work in the office was interesting because I like it.
To attend to telephone lines and keep two sets of books, and so on.
And everything was good until one day, when Mr.
Tannenbaum's brother-in-law wanted to change, rather to be not only a storeroom keeper, but he wanted to go out in the city as a salesman.
And Mr.
Tannenbaum asked me to help him to cut material.
We had material for ladies costumes, and skirts, and so on.
And with dirty hands, I said, I can't work in the office here or there.
I will work for you from 9:00 to 5:00.
But from 5:00 to 6:00, I go to the storeroom.
We'll prepare the parcels.
And because I said, you have to give me one pound more for this.
And he said, we can't increase your wage.
So I found that he's too hard for me.
So I was looking for another job and another job.
And so eventually, I came to the last job.
And I was working 17 years with Mr.
Millstein. ?] It was a well-known factory, Palm Beach Fashions, swimsuits.
And I met there his brother-in-law, Mr.
Lane, who was also known here.
He had also a factory.
Half of the building was belonging to Palm Beach.
One was belonging to Lane Swimwear.
So I worked half a day here and half a day there.
Yes.
The son has got, already, four or five children, [?
Kevin. ?] Yes.
No, Mr.
Millstein ?] was childless.
Oh, I had one son.
Yes.
Yes.
Good.
Yes.
